title: Dark Sky API Vocabulary
description: Domain vocabulary and glossary for the Dark Sky Forecast API.

terms.definition: An object containing various properties each representing the average (or maximum, or minimum) of a particular weather phenomenon occurring during a period of time. All properties are optional.
An object containing a human-readable summary and an array of data points for a given time period (minutely, hourly, or daily).
A data point containing the current weather conditions at the requested location.
A data block containing minute-by-minute weather data for the next hour. Not always available.
A data block containing hour-by-hour weather data for the next 48 hours (or 168 hours when extended).
A data block containing day-by-day weather data for the next 7 days.
An array of alert objects representing severe weather alerts issued for the requested location by governmental weather authorities.
An object containing metadata about the response, including the list of weather data sources used.
A Dark Sky API request that returns historical or forecast weather data for a specific point in time identified by a Unix timestamp or ISO 8601 datetime.
A predicted weather condition for a future time, as distinguished from current or historical observed conditions.
Weather data with very high geographic precision (down to 0.001° of latitude and longitude), enabling block-level forecasts rather than city-wide averages.
The intensity of precipitation in the requested units (inches/hour for us/uk2/ca; mm/hour for si). A value of 0 indicates no precipitation.
The probability of precipitation occurring, expressed as a value between 0 and 1 (inclusive).
The type of precipitation occurring. Possible values: rain, snow, sleet. Only defined when precipIntensity > 0.
The amount of snowfall accumulation expected during the period, in inches (us/uk2/ca) or centimeters (si).
A representation of the apparent (or 'feels like') temperature, accounting for wind chill (when temperature < 50°F) or heat index (when temperature > 80°F), in the requested units.
The dew point — the temperature to which air must be cooled to become saturated with water vapor — in the requested units.
The relative humidity as a number between 0 and 1 (inclusive).
The sea-level air pressure in millibars.
The wind speed in mph (us/uk2), km/h (ca), or m/s (si).
The wind gust speed — a short burst of wind speed significantly higher than the average wind speed — in the requested units.
The direction that the wind is coming from in degrees, with true north at 0° and progressing clockwise. 90° = east, 180° = south, 270° = west.
The percentage of sky occluded by clouds, as a value between 0 and 1 (inclusive).
The UV index — a measure of the ultraviolet radiation intensity from the sun that can cause skin damage.
The average visibility in miles (us/uk2) or kilometers (si/ca), capped at 10 miles/km.
The columnar density of total atmospheric ozone at the given time in Dobson units.
The fractional part of the lunation number during the given day. 0 = new moon, 0.25 = first quarter, 0.5 = full moon, 0.75 = last quarter. Only available on daily data points.

A machine-readable text summary of the weather conditions suitable for selecting an icon for display. Values include: clear-day, clear-night, rain, snow, sleet, wind, fog, cloudy, partly-cloudy-day, partly-cloudy-night.
A human-readable text summary of the weather conditions. This summary is designed to be displayed to the user as-is.
The unit system used for the response. Options: auto (select by location), us (imperial), si (metric), ca (SI but km/h for wind), uk2 (SI but miles for visibility and mph for wind).
A comma-separated list of data blocks to omit from the response (currently, minutely, hourly, daily, alerts, flags). Excluding unneeded blocks reduces latency.
When set to 'hourly', the API returns hourly data for 168 hours (7 days) instead of the default 48 hours.
The language to use for human-readable text summaries. Supports over 50 languages using BCP 47 language codes.
A type of weather alert indicating that conditions may cause significant inconvenience and, if caution is not exercised, could lead to situations that may threaten life or property.
A type of weather alert indicating that conditions are favorable for the occurrence of a hazardous weather event, but the occurrence, location, or timing is still uncertain.
A type of weather alert indicating that a hazardous weather or hydrologic event is occurring, is imminent, or has a very high probability of occurring.
The approximate distance to the nearest storm in the requested units. A storm distance of 0 doesn't necessarily mean it's raining; it could be drizzling nearby.
The approximate direction of the nearest storm in degrees, with true north at 0° and progressing clockwise. Only returned in the currently data point.
